title: Relative quantum product and open WDVV equations
speaker: Sara Tukachinsky
abstract: The standard WDVV equations are PDEs in the potential function that generates Gromov-Witten invariants. These equations imply relations on the invariants, and sometimes allow computations thereof, as demonstrated by Kontsevich-Manin (1994). We prove analogous equations for open Gromov-Witten invariants that we defined in a previous work. For ($\mathbb CP^n$ ,$\mathbb RP^n$), the resulting relations allow the computation of all invariants. The formulation of the open WDVV requires a lift of the big quantum product to relative cohomology.

proof_stages.label: Open A-infinity algebra from holomorphic disks
proof_stages.summary: The speaker fixes the symplectic/Lagrangian hypotheses, coefficient rings, grading conventions, bulk class gamma, and disk moduli spaces, then defines the pull-push operations m_k^gamma and states that they satisfy the A-infinity relations.

proof_stages.label: Bounding pairs and Maurer-Cartan equation
proof_stages.summary: A bounding pair (gamma,b) is defined by a Maurer-Cartan equation m^gamma(e^b)=c·1, and in the rational homology sphere case such pairs modulo gauge equivalence are classified by the cohomology class of gamma together with the integral of b over L.

proof_stages.label: Superpotential as open Gromov-Witten generating function
proof_stages.summary: The talk adds the zero-boundary-point operation m_{-1}, separates spherical-disk terms by a type-D projection, defines the gauge-invariant superpotential Omega(gamma,b), and for a natural choice of gamma and b identifies its coefficients with genus-zero open Gromov-Witten invariants.

proof_stages.label: Need for a relative quantum product
proof_stages.summary: By comparing with the usual closed WDVV degeneration of a four-pointed sphere, the speaker explains that open WDVV requires an analogue of the quantum product whose inputs live in relative cohomology.

proof_stages.label: Relative quantum complex as a closed-open cone
proof_stages.summary: Using the bounding pair to deform the Floer differential on forms on L, the speaker defines the closed-open map from the closed quantum complex to the open complex and takes its cone as the relative quantum complex.

proof_stages.label: Geodesic correction operations for the module structure
proof_stages.summary: The usual quantum product in the closed component fails to commute with the closed-open map on chains, and this failure is measured by geodesic-constrained disk operations that supply the correction terms for a module structure on the relative complex.

proof_stages.label: Reduced relative product lifting the absolute product
proof_stages.summary: After passing to a reduced relative complex and adjusting the absolute and relative parts, the corrected module operation becomes a relative quantum product that lifts the ordinary absolute quantum product and is meant to control interior bubbling in open WDVV.

examples.name: Standard pair CP^n with RP^n
examples.note: The standard real Lagrangian in complex projective space is given as a concrete case satisfying the regularity/orientability assumptions and later as a case where the relevant cohomology degrees are even.
examples.engagement_reason: CP^n with RP^n is named as the base/standard example and a source of even-degree cohomology, but no construction or computation is carried out specifically for it.

passes.reason: The standard CP^n/RP^n pair is a homogeneous highly regular model example, not an obstruction.
the standard CP^n/RP^n pair is a highly regular homogeneous symmetric model, used as a base example rather than against a claim
The standard CP^n/RP^n pair is a homogeneous rank-one symmetric model used as the base example, not to refute a claim.
The standard pair (CP^n,RP^n) is used as a homogeneous, highly regular model satisfying the assumptions, not to refute a claim.
The standard pair \((\mathbb{CP}^n,\mathbb{RP}^n)\) is used as a canonical highly homogeneous model satisfying the assumptions, not to refute anything.
the standard CP^n/RP^n pair is a homogeneous maximally regular running model, not used to refute a claim
the standard pair is used as a base example satisfying the hypotheses and is distinguished by its homogeneous projective/real-locus symmetry
Used as the standard running pair satisfying the hypotheses, and structurally it is the highly homogeneous projective space with its standard real form.
Used as the standard homogeneous real-form model satisfying the hypotheses, not to refute a claim.
The standard CP^n/RP^n pair is a highly homogeneous regular infinite-family model used as the running positive example.
The standard pair \(\mathbb{CP}^n,\mathbb{RP}^n\) is used as a basic homogeneous example satisfying the hypotheses.
Used as the standard base example where the assumptions hold, and CP^n with RP^n is a highly homogeneous symmetric pair.

examples.name: Four-point closed WDVV degeneration
examples.note: The familiar degeneration of a sphere with four marked constraints is used to show why, in the open setting, one needs a relative lift of the usual quantum product.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker traces the four-point WDVV degeneration, describing which marked points collide, the resulting nodal configurations, and how these boundary terms relate to quantum products.

passes.reason: It is a nodal boundary degeneration of a four-marked sphere used to explain WDVV, not to refute a claim.
the WDVV nodal degeneration is a collapsed boundary stratum of the four-pointed sphere moduli space, used to explain an equality
The four-point WDVV configuration is a nodal boundary degeneration of the moduli of marked spheres, used to explain a relation rather than a failure.
The four-point WDVV nodal degeneration is a boundary/limiting stable-curve stratum with collapsed nodal structure, used to derive equality of boundary contributions.
The four-point WDVV nodal degeneration is a boundary limiting stable-curve configuration arising from collision of marked points, used to explain equality of boundary contributions.
the four-marked sphere splitting is a nodal boundary degeneration used to explain the WDVV relation
the WDVV object is a nodal boundary degeneration of the four-point genus-zero moduli problem, not an adversarial example
Used to illustrate the usual WDVV boundary relation, and the object is a nodal boundary degeneration of a smooth four-marked sphere.
A nodal collision of four marked points is a boundary degeneration used to derive WDVV relations, not a failure case.
The WDVV collision is a nodal boundary degeneration of a four-pointed sphere used to derive an equality, not to refute a claim.
The four-point WDVV nodal splitting is a boundary degeneration of the moduli space used as a motivating model, not as a failure case.
Used to recall the usual WDVV boundary equality, and the nodal two-component curve is a singular boundary degeneration of the smooth four-pointed sphere.

examples.name: Reduced singular cohomology analogy
examples.note: Reduced cohomology in ordinary singular cohomology is invoked as an analogy for modifying the relative complex so that the module operation becomes a product.
examples.engagement_reason: Reduced singular cohomology is invoked only as an analogy for defining a reduced relative complex, without working through the singular cohomology case.

passes.reason: Reduced singular cohomology is invoked as a standard analogy for the reduced complex construction, not as a special or adversarial object.
reduced singular cohomology is invoked as a standard analogy for the reduced relative complex, not as a special or adversarial case
Reduced singular cohomology is a standard systematic analogy for the reduced relative complex, not an adversarial example.
